Client 2: Net or gross? Because after taxes it’s way less.
Advisor: Good question—gross, before taxes. We’ll handle taxes separately.
Client 1: Gross household is about $106,100 a year.
Client 2: Yeah, that sounds right—around $106k.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to split it out so I understand sources—Josep, are you working right now or is it more pension-type income?

Advisor: Perfect. Okay—going back to what you said about month-to-month stress, let’s anchor on cash flow first. Roughly what are your total monthly expenses, all-in?
Client 2: Uh… like six thousand? Maybe a little more.
Client 1: It’s higher than that. Closer to $6,800 a month, all-in.
Client 2: Right—sorry, I was thinking just the bills, not everything.
Advisor: Got it—common. When you say “all-in,” Josep, that $6,800 includes groceries, utilities, insurance, the whole thing?

Advisor: Okay, I’m writing down $6,800 per month for total expenses. Now within that, what’s the monthly debt payment—like mortgage, loans—what’s the required minimum each month?
Client 2: The mortgage is… $1,500?
Client 1: It’s $1,650 a month. Not $1,500.
Client 2: Okay, $1,650. I always round it in my head.
Advisor: No worries. So monthly debt cost total is $1,650—basically the mortgage. Any other debt payments besides that?

Advisor: Great. Now I want to shift to assets—what you’ve built up that’s investable. If you had to estimate your total investable assets—so not the house—what’s the combined total?
Client 2: I thought it was like… five hundred thousand?
Client 1: It’s more than that. About $606,800 total investable.
Client 2: Wait, does that include my 401(k) and that private thing?
Advisor: Yes—401(k)/IRA, taxable accounts, bank/investment accounts, private investments—basically anything that’s not the primary residence. Not the house value.

Client 2: Yes, that’s the one—about $81,400.
Advisor: Great. Now, separate from investable assets, you also have your home. What’s the current value you’d put on the primary residence?
Client 1: About $579,950.
Advisor: And the mortgage balance outstanding—what’s left on it?
Client 2: Like three hundred thousand?

Advisor: And the mortgage balance outstanding—what’s left on it?
Client 2: Like three hundred thousand?
Client 1: It’s $329,350 outstanding.
Advisor: $329,350—thank you. And the interest rate on that mortgage, do you know it offhand?
Client 1: 4.75%.

Advisor: And the mortgage balance outstanding—what’s left on it?
Client 2: Like three hundred thousand?
Client 1: It’s $329,350 outstanding.
Advisor: $329,350—thank you. And the interest rate on that mortgage, do you know it offhand?
Client 1: 4.75%.

Advisor: Perfect. Okay—going back to what you said about month-to-month stress, let’s anchor on cash flow first. Roughly what are your total monthly expenses, all-in?
Client 2: Uh… like six thousand? Maybe a little more.
Client 1: It’s higher than that. Closer to $6,800 a month, all-in.
Client 2: Right—sorry, I was thinking just the bills, not everything.
Advisor: Got it—common. When you say “all-in,” Josep, that $6,800 includes groceries, utilities, insurance, the whole thing?

Advisor: Hi Josep, hi Linda—nice to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what made you decide to come in now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Mostly timing—retirement planning, and I’ve got ongoing obligations from my prior marriage, so I want the plan to be… resilient.
Client 2: And I just want it to feel less stressful month to month. Like, we’re fine, but it doesn’t always feel fine.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Josep, Linda—quick housekeeping: is it just the two of you in the household, two adults?
Client 2: Yes, just two adults.

Client 1: Right, zero dependents. The alimony is an obligation, but not a dependent.
Advisor: Got it—so dependents count is 0. Thanks for separating that out.
Advisor: Next one’s taxes. Josep, you’d mentioned you’re in a higher bracket—are we talking about the 35% federal bracket for you two?
Client 1: Yes, 35% is the bracket we usually land in.
Client 2: Wait—35% is like what comes out of every paycheck, right?

Client 1: Yeah—second marriage for me. Linda, first for you.
Client 2: Well—technically I was married very briefly years ago, Josep.
Client 1: Oh—right, sorry. I always forget that. But for the household, we’re the “secondly wedded” situation, yeah.
Advisor: Thanks for the correction, Linda. So we’ll call the household marital status “secondly wedded.”
Advisor: Last housekeeping item: what state are you living in now? Just the state—no address.

Client 1: It’s $1,650 a month. Not $1,500.
Client 2: Okay, $1,650. I always round it in my head.
Advisor: No worries. So monthly debt cost total is $1,650—basically the mortgage. Any other debt payments besides that?
Client 1: No, just the mortgage.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk income next. What do you have coming in over a year, gross—before taxes? Ballpark is fine and we’ll tighten it.

Client 1: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Yeah, I hear you—spring can’t decide what it’s doing. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about the pension being the big one, I want to zoom out for a second.
Advisor: Josep and Linda, when markets get choppy, how would you describe your overall risk comfort—conservative, moderate, aggressive… what feels right?
Client 1: Moderate. I’m not trying to swing for the fences, but I don’t want everything in cash either.
Client 2: Yeah, moderate. I can handle some ups and downs as long as the bills are covered.

Client 2: Uh… I mean what I take home.
Client 1: No, Linda—he’s asking gross. Your gross is about twenty-five three, right?
Client 2: Oh—right, sorry. Gross is about $25,350 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thanks for the correction. So Linda, gross annual income about $25,350.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m labeling you both correctly—Josep, you’re Client 1, and Linda, you’re Client 2. Is that right?

Client 1: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yep.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, Linda, in the household setup, I have you as the spouse/partner—does that description fit how you want it reflected?
Client 2: Yes, spouse/partner is right.
Client 1: Yeah, that’s accurate.

Client 1: Yeah, that’s accurate.
Advisor: Okay. Next one’s a little “form-ish,” but helpful for planning—Linda, what’s your date of birth?
Client 2: It’s September 12th, 1983.
Advisor: September 12, 1983—thank you.
Client 2: Wait—do you need the year? I always feel weird saying it out loud.

Client 2: Okay, yeah—1983.
Advisor: Great.
Advisor: And what do you do for work, Linda? Like the broad category—employee versus business owner, that kind of thing.
Client 2: I’m basically a business owner.
Client 1: Well—she’s not “basically,” she is. It’s her company.

Advisor: Thanks. And just to split it out so I understand sources—Josep, are you working right now or is it more pension-type income?
Client 1: I’m not working. It’s pension income coming in monthly.
Advisor: Monthly pension—okay. And Linda, you’re still employed, right?
Client 2: Yes, I’m working.
Advisor: Great. Now I want to shift to assets—what you’ve built up that’s investable. If you had to estimate your total investable assets—so not the house—what’s the combined total?

Client 2: Oh—yeah, I mixed that up. Sorry.

Client 2: Like three hundred thousand?

Advisor: $329,350—thank you. And the interest rate on that mortgage, do you know it offhand?
Client 1: 4.75%.

Client 1: It’s later—final payment date is September 2043.
Advisor: September 2043—got it. Okay, last category: protection. Josep, do you have any long-term care coverage in place?
Client 1: Yes, I have an LTC policy.
Advisor: What’s the monthly premium on that, roughly?
Client 2: Is premium the amount it pays you?

Advisor: Now, one more detail on that policy: does it have an “assured until” date—like coverage guaranteed through a certain date—or is it lifetime? I’m asking because some of these have a specific end date.
Client 2: Isn’t it until he’s, like, 65? Or… I don’t know.
Client 1: No, it’s a date. It says assured until May 15th, 2052.
Advisor: May 15, 2052—thank you. Linda, you were thinking age-based, which is common, but this one’s written as a calendar date.
Client 2: Okay, that makes sense now.

Advisor: Hi Josep, hi Linda—nice to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what made you decide to come in now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Mostly timing—retirement planning, and I’ve got ongoing obligations from my prior marriage, so I want the plan to be… resilient.
Client 2: And I just want it to feel less stressful month to month. Like, we’re fine, but it doesn’t always feel fine.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Josep, Linda—quick housekeeping: is it just the two of you in the household, two adults?
